headline: UN Names El Obeid Before the Assault
deck: Investigators found the RSF's El Fasher atrocities bear the markers of genocide, and in a 6 July resolution passed without a vote the Human Rights Council opened an urgent inquiry into El Obeid, a city the RSF has now encircled. The wager is that naming a crime in advance can raise its cost.
body: United Nations investigators put a name to what happened in El Fasher. The Fact-Finding Mission on Sudan reported that mass killings, abductions and gang rapes carried out by the Rapid Support Forces when they seized the city bear the markers of genocide, and its chair, Mohamed Chande Othman, called the documented pattern of encirclement, attacks on civilian infrastructure and abuses against civilians "a stark warning" [E1]. The two-year war behind that finding has killed at least 59,000 people and displaced some 14 million [E1].

The Council did not stop at the past tense. In a resolution adopted without a vote on 6 July, it directed the mission to open an urgent inquiry into El Obeid, where the RSF has encircled the city, cut critical infrastructure and restricted essential services, the same tactics catalogued at El Fasher [E1]. Mona Rishmawi, an expert on the mission, drew the line plainly: "El Obeid must not become the next crime scene" [E1].

Europe attached its weight to the same warning. In its statement to the urgent debate, the European Union invoked its own report, "Sudan: Hallmarks of Genocide in El-Fasher," and said the patterns documented there "must not be repeated in El Obeid" [E2]. The language is deliberately pre-emptive, an attempt to spend institutional credibility before an assault rather than to condemn one after it.

Whether that spends anything is the open question. An inquiry launched in advance is the Council's strongest instrument and, at the same time, its weakest, because it names a crime it cannot physically prevent [E1][E2]. The RSF took El Fasher through an 18-month siege while the world watched and documented; a resolution in Geneva did not lift that siege [E1]. The useful test is not the wording but the effect: whether the 6 July action produces any material change, a humanitarian corridor, a named cost, a supplier put on notice, or only a fuller record for a later tribunal.

The grim reading should be held loosely. Encirclement is not proof that El Obeid will repeat El Fasher; sieges sometimes end in withdrawal or negotiated access, and the RSF has an incentive to avoid a second documented genocide finding [E1]. What would falsify the worst case is concrete, RSF-permitted aid routes or a halt to the tightening. What would confirm it is the opposite, and the Council has now put its name on the outcome either way [E1][E2].

dissent.argument: Naming in advance rarely moves a militia mid-campaign. The RSF completed the El Fasher siege under full documentation; absent enforcement or a supply cutoff, the El Obeid inquiry is most likely an evidentiary record, not a deterrent.

evidence_box.fragment: El Fasher atrocities bear the markers of genocide; 6 July resolution launches urgent El Obeid inquiry; RSF encircled the city; "El Obeid must not become the next crime scene"
EU statement to the HRC urgent debate: patterns in the "Hallmarks of Genocide in El-Fasher" report must not be repeated in El Obeid
